description:
You are a data-analyst who has just received a very small list of IP
    addresses that need an immediate network-reachability check.  
    A colleague has already saved the list for you in a CSV file:

        /home/user/data/targets.csv

    The file has exactly three comma-separated columns (no spaces):

        hostname,description
        127.0.0.1,local loopback
        192.0.2.1,test unreachable host

    Your job is to:

    1. Launch *parallel* reachability tests (use any standard Linux
       user-space tool, e.g. ping with a small packet count) for every
       host in the CSV.  Every test *must* be started in the background so
       that the individual commands are running concurrently, not one
       after the other.

    2. Wait until all background tests finish.

    3. Produce a concise summary CSV named

           /home/user/output/diagnostics_summary.csv

       The summary file MUST:

       • Be pure ASCII text, newline-terminated (LF) and contain **exactly
         four comma-separated fields** in the order shown below.
       • Contain a header line followed by one data line per host, in the
         same order that they appeared in targets.csv.
       • Use the literal strings “reachable” or “unreachable” (all
         lowercase, no quotes) in the Status column, derived from the exit
         status of the reachability command (success ⇒ reachable, failure
         ⇒ unreachable).
       • Use ISO-8601 date format (YYYY-MM-DD) for the Date column.
       • Look *exactly* like this (apart from the actual, current date):

           hostname,description,status,date
           127.0.0.1,local loopback,reachable,2023-11-18
           192.0.2.1,test unreachable host,unreachable,2023-11-18

         (replace “2023-11-18” with today’s real date inside the
         container, but keep the rest identical).

    4. Create a plain-text diagnostic log named

           /home/user/logs/diagnostics.log

       Each test must append exactly one line to this log, *in the exact
       order the tests complete*, using the format

           [YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS] <hostname> <status>

       Example (timestamps will differ, of course):

           [2023-11-18 14:07:01] 127.0.0.1 reachable
           [2023-11-18 14:07:04] 192.0.2.1 unreachable

       • The square brackets are mandatory.
       • Timestamp must be in 24-hour, second-precision local time.
       • Use one single space between the three fields.

    5. Ensure that the directory /home/user/output and the directory
       /home/user/logs exist.  Create them if they do not already exist,
       with default user permissions (you do not have root).

    6. When everything is done, you may print the contents of
       diagnostics_summary.csv to stdout so the user can see the result,
       but the real verification will read the files directly from disk.

    Important verification hints:
    • The automated checker will confirm that both output files exist,
      have the correct names, and match the *exact* formats described
      above (header, field order, field count, spelling, lowercase letters
      where required, newline at end of file, etc.).
    • The checker will specifically test that the Status column contains
      “reachable” for 127.0.0.1 and “unreachable” for 192.0.2.1.

    You do NOT need root privileges at any point.  Avoid hard-coding any
    absolute paths other than the ones specified above so the script
    remains portable inside the container.

    Deliverables to be present when you finish:
    1. /home/user/output/diagnostics_summary.csv  – formatted exactly as
       specified.
    2. /home/user/logs/diagnostics.log            – one line per tested
       host in completion order.

    No other files are required.
truth:
Initial files/directories before the task starts
    -----------------------------------------------
    1. File: /home/user/data/targets.csv
       Contents (including header line and trailing newline):
           hostname,description
           127.0.0.1,local loopback
           192.0.2.1,test unreachable host

    Expected files/directories after successful completion
    ------------------------------------------------------
    1. Directory: /home/user/output            (permissions 0755)
    2. Directory: /home/user/logs              (permissions 0755)

    3. File: /home/user/output/diagnostics_summary.csv  
       • Must exist and be readable.  
       • First line (header) must be exactly:
             hostname,description,status,date\n
       • Second line must be exactly:
             127.0.0.1,local loopback,reachable,<TODAY>\n
       • Third line must be exactly:
             192.0.2.1,test unreachable host,unreachable,<TODAY>\n
       • <TODAY> equals the container’s current local date in YYYY-MM-DD.

    4. File: /home/user/logs/diagnostics.log  
       • Must exist and be readable.  
       • Must contain exactly two lines, newline-terminated.  
       • Each line must match the regex  
             ^\[[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2} [0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}\] (127\.0\.0\.1|192\.0\.2\.1) (reachable|unreachable)$  
       • The line mentioning 127.0.0.1 must contain “reachable”.  
       • The line mentioning 192.0.2.1 must contain “unreachable”.

    The testing harness will:
    • Confirm the two required directories exist.
    • Read diagnostics_summary.csv and verify:
        – Exactly 3 lines (header + 2 data lines).
        – Correct field count (4), correct header text, correct ordering.
        – Lowercase status strings as specified.
    • Read diagnostics.log and verify:
        – Exactly 2 lines, matching regex above.
        – Correct host ↔ status mapping.
        – Lines appear in the order they were appended by the running
          diagnostic commands (not necessarily the IP order; the test
          accepts either order but checks that there are two distinct
          lines with the correct host/status combinations).

    The harness will treat any deviation (wrong filename, wrong header,
    missing newline, extra spaces, capital letters, additional columns,
    etc.) as a failure.
